text:
PARIS — For as long asRyan Crouserhas spun in a shot put ring, superlatives have described his career.

Standing 6-foot-7, with an enormous reach, he was often the biggest among his competitors. His analytical mind harnessed that frame to throw the 16-pound metal ball farther than anyone in history in 2021 — then reinvented his technique to do it again last year.

After Saturday night in Paris, when Crouser earned an unprecedented third consecutiveOlympicgold medal inside Stade de France, it is time to update the bio.

The most accomplished shot putter ever? The most dominant?

Both apply to the 31-year-old from Boring, who first displayed his potential at Gresham’s Barlow High School before taking his feats global.

With a winning throw of 75 feet, 1¾ inches, Crouser capped his most challenging season with perhaps his most rewarding victory under the lights and pressure from his body, but never pressure from competitors. After enduring through an ulnar nerve injury in his throwing elbow in March, and a pectoral muscle tear in April, he arrived in Paris coming off of his first loss in 10 months yet still believing that “at the top end I can still be as good as I ever was, if not better,” he said this week.

He backed it up during the final. His first-attempt throw of 74 feet, 3½ inches would have been far enough to win on its own, but then he improved on his next two throws.

“The self-doubt, the injuries that went into this makes me appreciate it all the more,” Crouser said.

American Joe Kovacs earned the silver medal and Brazil’s Rajindra Campbell took the bronze, with each throwing 72-8.

By the fourth round of throws, a light rain began falling that turned the ring slick and left competitors such as Leonardo Fabbri, the Italian who held the world lead for portions of the season and dealt Crouser a defeat in July in London, losing their footing.

Crouser adjusted his own technique in the rain to avoid slipping, going away from the “Crouser slide” that had helped him become the only man to ever throw past 77 feet when he set the world record of 77-3¾.

Saturday’s win broke the consecutive-gold tie he held with shot putters Ralph Rose (1904, 1908), Parry O’Brien (1952, 1956) and Tomasz Majewski (2008, 2012).

The technique he used on his winning throw was one Crouser himself invented in December 2023 while tinkering in the facility where he coaches himself in Fayetteville, Arkansas. And that reinvention of his technique came after he’d already won Olympic gold medals in Rio de Janeiro and Tokyo, yet Crouser was unconvinced he had thrown his farthest. It was a risky move, but a puzzle a man with economics and finance degrees set out to solve.

His challenge this year was even more difficult — overcoming injuries serious enough that they left him seriously wondering whether he could even make the U.S. team, let alone defend his gold medals.

“Being the first to win three is a testament to just how much the shot does beat you up,” Crouser said. “It’s been a total commitment. I think being Olympic champ for three consecutive is kind of a testament to the total dedication and hard work that has gone into it.”

Those accomplishments have earned Crouser adoration in Oregon. Gresham threw the Barlow High School grad a hero’s welcome after his first gold medal, in Rio de Janeiro. Following his second, in 2021, it officially celebrated his birthday. Gresham wasn’t just the backdrop to Crouser’s rise within shot put; it also sparked it. While in fourth grade, Crouser watched the American medalists Adam Nelson and Reese Hoffa compete at a meet in the city. By 2016, he had usurped them as the top U.S. thrower by winning the U.S. Olympic trials.

RELATED:Barlow community watches Crouser win gold

Few have been able to consistently defeat Crouser since, as he won Olympic gold in Rio de Janeiro and Tokyo, plus world championships in 2022 and 2023. In 2021, he set a world record that he improved upon last year. It has made him consider transitioning to discus. Doing so in 2026 could be a possibility.

“It’s a bit of a walking a tightrope between, I’d like to throw discus, but I can’t take away too much from shot,” Crouser said. “Because the goal for me would be to retire in 2028 as an American (athlete) retiring on American soil and the Olympics would be a dream come true.”

His health will have to hold up to get there. Starting when two blood clots were discovered in a leg before the 2023 world championships — which were not enough to keep him from winning, however — Crouser has endured multiple health issues in the past 12 month that culminated with the elbow and pec injuries. The injuries had come, in part, from his realization that aging would not allow him to continue with the same type of intensity as earlier in his career. He spent the previous month before Saturday attempting to throw more often, but at a lighter intensity, because hard throwing days forced him to take multiple days to recover.

Crouser said there were “a lot of unknowns” physically when he woke up Saturday whether he could hit the mark he believed he would need to win against a field featuring three competitors who rank in the top-10 throwers all-time.

When Crouser entered the stadium for his introduction prior to the final, he bent a knee and posed as “a little shout out to a French sculptor,” he said, mimicking the timeless Rodin sculpture, “The Thinker.”

He left the night, once again, as shot put’s own enduring classic. The victor.

-- Andrew Greif for The Oregonian/OregonLive
